name: Jharkhand

overview.short: Jharkhand — "Land of Forests" — was carved from Bihar in 2000 to give its primarily tribal population (26% of the state is Scheduled Tribe, the largest proportion in East India) political self-determination. The Chota Nagpur Plateau, a mineral-rich highland with extraordinary waterfalls, dense forests, and flowing rivers, is the state's defining geography.
overview.paragraph2: India's mineral heartland, Jharkhand holds 40% of India's mineral reserves — coal, iron ore, copper, uranium, mica — beneath its red laterite soils. Yet this extractive industrial story sits alongside a rich tribal heritage: the Santali, Mundari, Ho, Kurux (Oraon), and Gond tribes maintain distinctive languages, festivals, art (Sohrai and Khovar cave paintings), and nature-worship traditions that are ancient and vibrant.
overview.paragraph3: The state's natural beauty is stunning — Hundru, Jonha, Dassam, and Hirni waterfalls tumble dramatically through forest gorges; Betla National Park is home to elephants and tigers; and the Parasnath Hill (Shikharji) — Jainism's most sacred pilgrimage — draws lakhs of white-clad pilgrims annually.
overview.cultureHeritage: Jharkhand's tribal culture is among India's richest and most alive. Sohrai and Khovar paintings — geometric and naturalistic wall paintings made by Santali women using natural pigments — are now internationally exhibited. The Chhau dance of Seraikela (a martial arts-based masked dance form) is a UNESCO Intangible Heritage of Humanity. Santali music (particularly the madal drum and flute) is extraordinary in its polyrhythmic complexity. The Sarhul (spring flower festival) and Karma festivals are celebrated with remarkable community joyfulness.

religiousPlaces.name: Shikharji (Parasnath)
religiousPlaces.significance: Jain's most sacred; 20 Tirthankara moksha sites

religiousPlaces.name: Jagannath Temple
religiousPlaces.significance: Puri replica; annual Rath Yatra; 17th century

rivers: Subarnarekha — "Gold-streaked" river; Hundru Falls; flows to Bay of Bengal
Damodar — Hazaribagh area; coal belt river
Koel — Betla NP; crocodile habitat; North and South Koel join to form Sankh/Brahmani
waterfalls: Dassam Falls — 44m; 10 streams; Kanchi River; monsoon spectacle
Jonha Falls — 43m; near Ranchi; seasonal; Johna waterfall
Hirni Falls — 37m; West Singhbhum; forest setting
Lower Ghaghri Falls — Latehar; dramatic gorge falls near Betla

wildlife.name: Betla NP
wildlife.note: 979 sq km; tiger, elephant; first Project Tiger

wildlife.name: Hazaribagh WLS
wildlife.note: 183 sq km; tiger, leopard; Hazaribagh plateau

wildlife.name: Dalma WLS
wildlife.note: Jamshedpur; elephant habitat; day trip from city

wildlife.name: Mahuadanr Wolf Sanctuary
wildlife.note: Latehar; India's only dedicated wolf sanctuary

historicalSites.name: Betla Fort
historicalSites.description: Inside Betla NP; 16th century; Chero dynasty; crumbling but atmospheric

historicalSites.name: Telkupi
historicalSites.description: Purulia (border); submerged temple complex exposed when dam level drops

historicalSites.name: Hazaribagh
historicalSites.description: British-era hill station; colonial architecture; pleasant climate; Rajrappa Temple nearby

historicalSites.name: Rajrappa
historicalSites.description: Ramgarh; Chhinnamasta Devi temple; waterfall at river confluence; powerful Shakti site

adventure: Betla Safari — Elephant back and jeep; early morning
Parasnath Trek — 24 km barefoot circuit; pilgrimage-adventure combination
Rock Climbing, Ranchi — Granite outcrops; weekend climbing
Topchanchi Lake Boating — Dhanbad district; scenic lake; forest background
Subarnarekha Rafting — Seasonal; near Hundru area

famousDishes.name: Rugra
famousDishes.description: Forest mushroom (wild variety); stir-fried; tribal delicacy

famousDishes.name: Dhuska
famousDishes.description: Deep-fried rice-lentil bread; crispy; Ranchi street staple

famousDishes.name: Pittha
famousDishes.description: Steamed rice dumplings; sweet or savory; festival food (same as Bihar)

famousDishes.name: Badi
famousDishes.description: Sun-dried lentil dumplings; added to curries and chutneys

drinks.name: Maad
drinks.description: Fermented rice beer; essential tribal beverage; Santali and Ho communities

drinks.name: Handia
drinks.description: Rice beer; similar to Maad; widespread in tribal areas

bestTimeToVisit.conditions: Waterfalls spectacular; forests lush; some roads difficult

airports: Birsa Munda Airport, Ranchi — Main airport; Delhi, Mumbai, Kolkata connections
railways: Ranchi, Dhanbad, Jamshedpur, Hazaribagh Road — Major junctions
Deoghar accessible from Jasidih Junction (on main Howrah-Delhi line)

festivals.name: Sarhul
festivals.description: Santali spring festival; Sal flower worship; community dance

festivals.name: Karma Festival
festivals.description: Brother-sister bond; nature worship; karmaah tree

festivals.name: Sohrai Festival
festivals.description: Cattle worship; wall paintings renewed; Hazaribagh district

travelTips: Deoghar Shravan: If visiting in July–August, be prepared for massive crowds; accommodation fills up completely; book weeks in advance
Tribal Areas: Seek local guidance for tribal village visits; always ask permission before photographing
Wildlife Booking: Betla safari online at jharkhand.gov.in
uniqueFacts: Jharkhand holds approximately 40% of India's total mineral wealth including coal, iron ore, copper, mica, and uranium
Mahuadanr Wolf Sanctuary in Latehar is India's only sanctuary exclusively dedicated to the wolf
The Chhau dance of Seraikela-Kharsawan district is on UNESCO's Intangible Cultural Heritage list
Sohrai and Khovar rock art at Isko (Hazaribagh) — some 5,000 years old — are still practiced by Santali women who paint their homes before festivals
Jharkhand's tribal communities speak 22 different recognized tribal languages
